titleStmt.title: Letter from Thomas Kirkpatrick to Madam, 29 March 1917
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Thomas Kirkpatrick

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: From 1910 until 1954 Thomas Percy Kirkpatrick (1869-1954) served as the registrar for the Royal College of Physicians of Ireland. He also served as the general secretary of the Royal Academy of Medicine in Ireland. He took a particular interest in what were then termed venereal diseases; to encourage his patients (many of whom were prostitutes) to attend, he held a clinic for women at a discreet early morning hour to facilitate anonymity.

msIdentifier.repository: Royal College of Physicians of Ireland

correspAction.placeName: Royal College of Physicians, Dublin, Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
ROYAL COLLEGE OF PHYSICIANS OF IRELAND,
6 Kildare-street, Dublin. 29th March, 1917. Madam, I am requested by the President and Fellows of this
College to forward you a copy of the following resolution which
was passed in solemn silence at a meeting of the College held
on Wednesday the 28th instant: --
Resolved : that the President and Fellows of the Royal College
of Physicians of Ireland have heard with profound regret of the
death of their respected colleague Dr. Henry Colpoys Tweedy, a
Fellow of the College since the year 1888, and they hereby
desire to convey to the members of his family and expression
of their deep sympathy with them in their bereavement. I am, Madam,
Yours faithfully,
Fellow & Registrar.
